social_history.alcohol: Rare, one to two beers per month

social_history.exercise: Very limited, walks to the mailbox with shortness of breath, uses a cane for balance

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention is intact; able to recite months of the year backwards without error. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion or post-ictal features noted at time of examination, approximately 45 minutes after the event.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. No papilledema on fundoscopy. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus, no diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in V1, V2, V3 bilaterally. Jaw strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in all extremities, no rigidity, spasticity, or cogwheeling. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups tested bilaterally: deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip strength, iliopsoas, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, gastrocnemius. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout. Pinprick intact. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Vibration moderately diminished at bilateral great toes and medial malleoli, consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Temperature sensation not tested.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally (diminished, consistent with peripheral neuropathy). Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly slow but steady gait with normal base. Uses cane for stability but walks independently. Tandem gait slightly unsteady but able to complete without falling. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of oral trauma. No urinary incontinence reported. A 3 cm contusion on the right parietal scalp from striking the kitchen counter during the fall. No signs of prolonged seizure activity.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden collapse at home without warning, witnessed by daughter
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Sofia Morales is a 66-year-old man with an extensive cardiac history including coronary artery disease status post CABG, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, chronic atrial flutter, hypertension, and diabetes who was brought to the emergency department by paramedics after a witnessed syncopal episode. According to her daughter, the patient was standing in the kitchen at approximately 10:30 AM when she abruptly collapsed to the floor without any warning. She reports that she did not cry out, did not grab onto anything, and appeared to simply 'drop like a stone.' She struck the right side of her head on the kitchen counter on the way down. Her daughter rushed to her side and found him unresponsive with her eyes open and a blank stare. She reports her face appeared ashen and her lips looked slightly blue. She was unresponsive for approximately 30 to 40 seconds. There were no convulsive movements, no tonic stiffening, no rhythmic jerking of the extremities, and no abnormal posturing during the episode. She then regained consciousness spontaneously and was immediately coherent, asking her daughter why she was on the floor next to him. There was no period of post-ictal confusion, drowsiness, or disorientation. She denied any tongue biting and there was no urinary incontinence. She reports that over the past two to three weeks she has been experiencing increasing episodes of lightheadedness and a sensation of her heart 'pausing' or 'skipping beats,' often occurring at rest. She has also had worsening exertional dyspnea and fatigue, now becoming short of breath simply walking from her bedroom to the bathroom. She had a similar but less severe episode five days ago where she nearly lost consciousness while getting up from a chair but caught herself on the armrest. She did not seek medical attention at that time. She denies chest pain, palpitations with rapid heart rate, fever, recent illness, or any new medications. Her daughter confirms that her CPAP compliance has been good. Her last cardiology follow-up was three months ago, at which time her carvedilol dose was increased from 12.5 mg to 25 mg twice daily because of suboptimal rate control of her atrial flutter.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: No sleep captured during this recording

artifacts.description: Prominent ECG artifact throughout recording reflecting underlying bradycardia, does not obscure cortical activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Mild diffuse slowing noted during hyperventilation that resolved within 30 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response observed. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake EEG recording. No epileptiform discharges, no focal abnormalities, and no seizures captured during this 30-minute routine study. The normal EEG does not support a diagnosis of epilepsy. Prominent ECG artifact reflecting severe bradycardia noted throughout the recording. Clinical correlation with cardiac evaluation is strongly recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording; single interictal EEG has limited sensitivity (approximately 50%) for detecting epileptiform discharges. No sleep was captured.
recommended_actions: Cardiac monitoring should be prioritized given clinical presentation and severe bradycardia noted on EEG artifact
If seizure suspicion persists despite cardiac diagnosis, consider prolonged EEG monitoring

ecg.rhythm: Complete heart block (third-degree AV block) with slow ventricular escape rhythm
findings: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial flutter waves at approximately 280 bpm
Slow ventricular escape rhythm at 32 bpm with wide QRS complex
QRS morphology consistent with left bundle branch block pattern in escape rhythm
Prolonged QTc at 510 ms, concerning for increased arrhythmic risk
ST-segment depression in leads I, aVL, V5, V6 (likely demand ischemia secondary to bradycardia)
No acute ST elevation
Occasional PVCs (2 seen on 12-lead tracing)
ecg.interpretation: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block with an underlying atrial flutter. The ventricular escape rhythm is wide complex (LBBB morphology) at a critically low rate of 32 bpm, indicating an infranodal block likely at the level of the bundle branches. The prolonged QTc of 510 ms raises concern for potential torsades de pointes. This ECG represents a life-threatening cardiac conduction abnormality that fully explains the patient's syncopal episode. Immediate cardiology consultation and preparation for emergent temporary pacing are required. The recent increase in carvedilol dose may have unmasked or worsened underlying conduction system disease.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Complete heart block with a very slow and unreliable ventricular escape rhythm is the definitive cause of this patient's syncope. The wide QRS escape complex suggests infranodal block, which carries a higher risk of asystole compared to narrow complex junctional escape. The prolonged QTc further increases arrhythmic risk. Combined with the patient's known reduced ejection fraction, this presentation carries significant morbidity and mortality risk without urgent intervention.

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Low, possibly from furosemide use; hypomagnesemia can contribute to arrhythmias and QTc prolongation

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results reveal no acute metabolic cause for the syncope, though several abnormalities are noted. Electrolytes are largely stable: potassium is mildly elevated at 5.1 mEq/L, which may contribute to conduction abnormalities and should be monitored closely given the combination of spironolactone, lisinopril, and CKD. Magnesium is low at 1.6 mg/dL, possibly from chronic furosemide use, and should be repleted as hypomagnesemia can exacerbate arrhythmias and prolong QTc. Creatinine is slightly above baseline, suggesting mild acute kidney injury from reduced cardiac output. Troponin is mildly elevated, consistent with demand ischemia from sustained severe bradycardia rather than acute coronary syndrome; serial monitoring is essential. BNP is significantly elevated, reflecting decompensated heart failure exacerbated by the hemodynamic insult of complete heart block. Mild normocytic anemia is present, likely multifactorial from CKD and chronic disease. INR is therapeutic on warfarin.

Echocardiogram.value: Moderate calcific aortic stenosis, peak gradient 32 mmHg, mean gradient 18 mmHg, AVA 1.3 cm2
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderate aortic stenosis with heavy calcification extending into the aortic root; calcification likely extending into the conduction system
Functional mitral regurgitation secondary to LV dilation and annular dilatation
Mildly reduced RV systolic function, TAPSE 14 mm
Elevated, suggests pulmonary hypertension secondary to left heart disease

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates moderately reduced left ventricular systolic function with LVEF of 35%, worsened from 40% six months prior. The LV is dilated. Moderate calcific aortic stenosis is present with heavy annular and valve calcification, which is a significant finding as this calcification likely extends into the conduction system and is the probable underlying cause of the complete heart block. Moderate functional mitral regurgitation secondary to LV dilation. Left atrium is moderately dilated. Mild RV dysfunction with elevated pulmonary artery pressures secondary to left heart disease. Given the LVEF of 35% and need for pacing, cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT-D) should be considered rather than a standard pacemaker.

Left ventricular end-diastolic diameter: 6.1 cm - Dilated, consistent with dilated cardiomyopathy from chronic ischemic and valvular heart disease
Left atrial diameter: 4.8 cm - Moderately dilated, consistent with chronic atrial flutter and elevated filling pressures
Interventricular septum thickness: 1.3 cm - Mildly thickened, consistent with longstanding hypertension

Aortic valve: Moderate calcific aortic stenosis, peak gradient 32 mmHg, mean gradient 18 mmHg, AVA 1.3 cm2 qualitative - Moderate aortic stenosis with heavy calcification extending into the aortic root; calcification likely extending into the conduction system
Mitral valve: Moderate regurgitation qualitative - Functional mitral regurgitation secondary to LV dilation and annular dilatation
Right ventricular function: Mildly reduced qualitative - Mildly reduced RV systolic function, TAPSE 14 mm
Estimated RVSP: 48 mmHg mmHg - Elevated, suggests pulmonary hypertension secondary to left heart disease

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with atrial flutter and slow ventricular escape throughout 24-hour recording
findings: Persistent complete AV block throughout entire 24-hour recording period
Continuous atrial flutter at 275-290 bpm with no conducted beats
Average ventricular escape rate 34 bpm (range 24-40 bpm)
Minimum ventricular rate 24 bpm at 02:18 AM with 5.8-second RR pause followed by escape beat
Maximum ventricular rate 40 bpm during ambulation to bathroom
Three episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (longest run 6 beats at 145 bpm)
Frequent PVCs: 342 total in 24 hours (approximately 2% burden)
Two episodes correlating with patient-reported lightheadedness and presyncope (during pauses)
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitor demonstrates persistent complete heart block with underlying atrial flutter. The ventricular escape rhythm is dangerously slow and unreliable, with a minimum rate of 24 bpm and a concerning 5.8-second pause during sleep. Three episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia were captured, raising concern for ventricular arrhythmia risk in the setting of prolonged QTc and structural heart disease. This is a high-risk recording mandating urgent permanent pacing. Given the reduced LVEF, NSVT, and ischemic cardiomyopathy, a CRT-D (cardiac resynchronization therapy with defibrillator) should be strongly considered.
output.clinical_correlation: The 5.8-second pause and persistent unreliable escape rhythm confirm that this patient is at imminent risk for recurrent syncope and sudden cardiac death. The presence of NSVT in the setting of ischemic cardiomyopathy with LVEF 35% further supports the indication for defibrillator capability in addition to pacing.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Low at baseline reflecting low cardiac output from complete heart block
Severely bradycardic from complete heart block
Significant orthostatic hypotension secondary to inability to augment cardiac output
No chronotropic response due to complete heart block

Tilt_table_test.value: Severe lightheadedness and near-syncope at 2 minutes, test aborted
Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Hemodynamic compromise from complete heart block with fixed low cardiac output
No vasovagal mechanism identified; orthostatic intolerance entirely attributable to complete heart block

output.interpretation: Tilt table test aborted early due to severe symptomatic hypotension. No vasovagal mechanism was identified. The hemodynamic compromise is entirely attributable to the patient's complete heart block with fixed, slow ventricular rate and inability to augment cardiac output with positional change. This test confirms that the syncope is cardiac in origin and not vasovagal.
abnormal_values_summary: Baseline supine BP: 98/56 mmHg - Low at baseline reflecting low cardiac output from complete heart block
Baseline supine HR: 33 bpm - Severely bradycardic from complete heart block
70-degree tilt BP at 1 min: 78/44 mmHg - Significant orthostatic hypotension secondary to inability to augment cardiac output
70-degree tilt HR at 1 min: 34 bpm - No chronotropic response due to complete heart block
Symptoms during tilt: Severe lightheadedness and near-syncope at 2 minutes, test aborted qualitative - Hemodynamic compromise from complete heart block with fixed low cardiac output

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block; escape rate did not increase with exercise
findings: Test terminated at Stage 1 of modified Bruce protocol after 1 minute 30 seconds due to symptomatic hypotension, severe dyspnea, and presyncope
Peak ventricular rate only 38 bpm (severe chronotropic incompetence from complete heart block)
Atrial flutter rate increased to 310 bpm but with persistent complete AV dissociation
Blood pressure fell from 96/54 to 72/40 at peak exercise
QTc prolonged further to 524 ms during exercise
2 mm horizontal ST depression in leads V4-V6 at peak exertion (demand ischemia)
One 4-beat run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia at test termination
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test was severely limited and terminated very early due to hemodynamic compromise. The patient demonstrated complete chronotropic incompetence due to persistent complete heart block. Significant ST depression and NSVT were provoked even at very low workload, indicating significant demand ischemia and arrhythmic risk. This test confirms the hemodynamic significance of the complete heart block and underscores the urgency of pacemaker implantation. Exercise testing was arguably contraindicated in this clinical setting.
output.clinical_correlation: The profound exercise intolerance, hemodynamic compromise, and provocation of NSVT during minimal exertion confirm the life-threatening nature of this conduction disturbance. Permanent pacing is urgently indicated. Given LVEF 35%, NSVT, and ischemic cardiomyopathy, CRT-D implantation is recommended.

findings.location: Basal interventricular septum and aortic root-septum junction

signal_characteristics.T1: Elevated native T1 values in basal septum (1180 ms)
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Dense mid-wall and subendocardial late gadolinium enhancement in the basal interventricular septum extending from the aortic annulus into the membranous septum, consistent with fibrocalcific degeneration

findings.location: Mid and apical anterior wall and apex (LAD territory)

signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Transmural subendocardial to full-thickness enhancement in LAD territory, consistent with chronic infarction
signal_characteristics.cine: Akinesis of mid-anterior wall and apex

signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Thin subendocardial enhancement consistent with prior non-transmural infarction

additional_observations: Heavily calcified aortic valve with reduced excursion
Moderate left atrial dilation (LA volume index 48 mL/m2)
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis or amyloidosis
No evidence of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
Aortic root calcification extending into the membranous septum
output.impression: Cardiac MRI reveals extensive fibrocalcific disease in the basal interventricular septum and aortic annulus-septum junction, which encompasses the His bundle and proximal bundle branches. This is consistent with calcific degenerative conduction system disease (Lev disease), with calcification from the sclerotic aortic valve extending into the conduction system. Additionally, there is evidence of extensive prior LAD territory infarction (transmural, approximately 25% of LV myocardium) and a smaller inferior territory infarction. LVEF is 35% with significant LV dilation. The combination of degenerative calcific conduction disease and ischemic cardiomyopathy provides the structural substrate for the complete heart block. CRT-D implantation is recommended given LVEF ≤35%, need for pacing, NYHA class II-III heart failure, and ischemic cardiomyopathy.
recommended_actions: CRT-D implantation given LVEF ≤35%, complete heart block, and ischemic cardiomyopathy
Aortic valve surveillance; current AS is moderate but may progress
Guideline-directed medical therapy optimization for heart failure
Close follow-up for potential future aortic valve intervention

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Calcific degenerative conduction system disease (Lev disease)
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Dense calcification extending from sclerotic aortic valve into the membranous septum and proximal conduction system
Prior extensive LAD territory infarction may have contributed to distal conduction system damage
No patchy mid-wall or epicardial enhancement pattern typical of sarcoidosis; enhancement pattern is consistent with ischemic and degenerative disease

CT_Head_Report.value: Moderate periventricular and subcortical white matter hypodensities
CT_Head_Report.clinical_significance: Chronic small vessel ischemic disease, more prominent than typical for age, consistent with poorly controlled hypertension and diabetes
Consistent with reported head strike during fall, no underlying fracture, important to monitor given therapeutic anticoagulation

output.interpretation: Non-contrast CT head shows no acute intracranial pathology. No intracranial hemorrhage, mass, or acute ischemic changes. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease, more prominent than age-expected, reflecting the patient's cardiovascular risk factors. Right parietal subgaleal hematoma from the fall is noted without underlying fracture. Given the patient's therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin (INR 2.3), repeat head CT in 6-12 hours should be considered to rule out delayed intracranial hemorrhage.

Calvarium: Right parietal subgaleal hematoma qualitative - Consistent with reported head strike during fall, no underlying fracture, important to monitor given therapeutic anticoagulation

findings.location: Left caudate head and right thalamus

additional_observations: No acute infarction on DWI/ADC sequences
Mild generalized cortical volume loss, slightly advanced for age

output.impression: Brain MRI demonstrates moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease and two chronic lacunar infarcts, consistent with the patient's cardiovascular risk factor profile (hypertension, diabetes, prior smoking). No acute intracranial pathology to explain the syncopal episode. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass lesion. The syncope etiology is not neurological based on these imaging findings.
recommended_actions: Primary evaluation should focus on cardiac etiology
Optimize cardiovascular risk factor management to slow progression of small vessel disease

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block with underlying calcific degenerative conduction system disease

differential.key_distinguishing: No tonic-clonic movements, no post-ictal confusion, immediate return to baseline alertness, no tongue bite, no urinary incontinence, normal EEG. The absence of any convulsive activity strongly argues against seizure.
No identifiable trigger (pain, prolonged standing, emotional stress), no prodromal nausea or diaphoresis, age 74 with extensive cardiac history makes vasovagal far less likely than cardiac etiology.

Patient collapsed while standing but not upon positional change; was already standing for several minutes. Multiple antihypertensive medications could contribute, but any orthostatic component is secondary to complete heart block.
No acute dyspnea or tachycardia, SpO2 94% at baseline consistent with known cardiopulmonary disease, complete heart block on ECG provides definitive alternative explanation.

Moderate aortic stenosis is present but the AVA of 1.3 cm2 is not severe enough to cause syncope independently; however, calcification from the aortic valve extending into the conduction system is the likely mechanism of the complete heart block.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately upon arrival in emergency department
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete heart block with slow ventricular escape at 32 bpm, wide QRS, prolonged QTc

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous cardiac telemetry monitoring, place transcutaneous pacing pads, and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Persistent severe bradycardia with AV dissociation on monitor

optimal_actions.action: Immediately hold carvedilol and all AV-nodal blocking agents
Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, coagulation studies, magnesium, TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly elevated troponin (demand), elevated BNP, low magnesium, mildly elevated potassium, creatinine above baseline

optimal_actions.action: Replete magnesium intravenously (2g magnesium sulfate) given level of 1.6 mg/dL and prolonged QTc
Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary transvenous pacing and permanent device planning
Obtain EEG to rule out epileptic seizure as cause of loss of consciousness
Obtain head CT to rule out intracranial hemorrhage from head trauma on anticoagulation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No intracranial hemorrhage, subgaleal hematoma from fall

optimal_actions.action: Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess cardiac structure and function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: LVEF 35%, moderate aortic stenosis, LV dilation

optimal_actions.action: Admit to cardiac care unit for monitoring and plan CRT-D implantation given LVEF ≤35%, complete heart block, and ischemic cardiomyopathy

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify complete heart block
Place transcutaneous pacing pads and have atropine at bedside
Immediately hold carvedilol and all AV-nodal blocking agents
Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary and permanent pacing
Replete magnesium given low level and prolonged QTc
Obtain head CT given head trauma on therapeutic anticoagulation
Recognize syncope is cardiac in origin and not a seizure
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs based on misdiagnosis of the syncopal episode as a seizure
Continuing carvedilol or any beta-blocker that further suppresses AV conduction and heart rate
Administering adenosine, which would cause asystole in the setting of complete heart block
Administering calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil) that suppress AV conduction
Discharging patient without cardiac monitoring, pacing evaluation, and head CT follow-up
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication, and patient is on therapeutic anticoagulation with INR 2.3)
Administering IV amiodarone for rate control (would further suppress escape rhythm)
key_reasoning_points: Sudden unheralded loss of consciousness without any warning, prodrome, or triggering event is the hallmark of cardiac syncope (Stokes-Adams attack)
The absence of tonic-clonic activity, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and urinary incontinence strongly argues against epileptic seizure

Immediate return to full alertness and coherent speech within seconds of regaining consciousness is characteristic of cardiac syncope; seizures typically produce post-ictal confusion lasting minutes to hours
The ECG showing complete heart block with wide-complex ventricular escape at 32 bpm provides the definitive diagnosis
The wide QRS escape morphology (LBBB pattern) indicates infranodal block, which is higher risk than narrow-complex junctional escape
The prolonged QTc of 510 ms, combined with hypomagnesemia, creates additional risk for torsades de pointes and must be addressed
The recent increase in carvedilol dose may have been the precipitant that unmasked or worsened underlying conduction system disease
The normal EEG confirms the non-epileptic nature of the transient loss of consciousness
The significantly elevated BNP (680 pg/mL) and worsened LVEF suggest that the chronic bradycardia from complete heart block has been decompensating the patient's heart failure
Head trauma on therapeutic anticoagulation (INR 2.3) requires head CT to rule out intracranial hemorrhage, with consideration for repeat imaging in 6-12 hours

clinical_pearls: Complete heart block with wide-complex escape indicates infranodal block and higher risk of asystole compared to narrow-complex junctional escape
Calcific aortic stenosis is a common cause of acquired complete heart block in the elderly through extension of calcification into the conduction system (Lev disease)
Head trauma in anticoagulated patients requires head CT even when the primary cause of the fall is identified, with consideration for delayed repeat imaging
Beta-blocker dose increases can unmask underlying conduction system disease that was previously compensated
LVEF ≤35% with need for ventricular pacing and heart failure favors CRT-D over standard pacemaker to prevent pacing-induced cardiomyopathy
